description: Eplerenone is a medication primarily used to treat high blood pressure (hypertension) and heart failure. It belongs to a class of drugs known as aldosterone antagonists. By blocking the effects of aldosterone, a hormone that can cause the body to retain sodium and water, eplerenone helps to lower blood pressure and reduce fluid buildup in the body. It is often prescribed following a heart attack or for conditions like chronic heart failure to improve heart function and prevent further damage.This model is a one-compartment pharmacokinetic (PK) model incorporating body weight, creatinine clearance, age, ethnicity and disease status (healthy or chronic heart failure) as covariates to account for interindividual variability in drug disposition.

- Species: Human
- Pathology: Hypertension and heart failure
- Biological context: The model was developed to characterize eplerenone pharmacokinetics (PK) in Japanese chronic heart failure patients and to estimate the impact of factors that may affect eplerenone PK, population pharmacokinetic.
- Virtual population characteristics: Parameters are given in the Example folder and were extracted from the manuscripts.
- Time scale: hours
- Biological scale: Whole body

description: Dapagliflozin is an oral antidiabetic medication that belongs to the class of SGLT2 inhibitors (sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 inhibitors). It lowers blood sugar levels in people with type 2 diabetes by preventing glucose reabsorption in the kidneys, leading to increased glucose excretion in the urine. Dapagliflozin is also used to treat heart failure and chronic kidney disease, offering cardiovascular and renal protection beyond its glucose-lowering effects.This model is a two-compartments pharmacokinetic (PK) model incorporating body weight, creatinine clearance, and gender as covariates to account for interindividual variability in drug disposition.

description: Bisoprolol is a beta-blocker used primarily to treat high blood pressure (hypertension) and heart-related conditions such as angina and heart failure. It works by slowing the heart rate and reducing the heart's workload, helping to lower blood pressure and improve heart efficiency. Bisoprolol is usually taken once daily in tablet form.This model is a one-compartment pharmacokinetic (PK) model incorporating body weight, creatinine clearance estimated by MDRD-4, and skeletal muscle mass index (SMI) as covariates to account for interindividual variability in drug disposition.

description:
Ivosidenib is an oral small molecule inhibitor that selectively targets and inhibits the mutant IDH1 (isocitrate dehydrogenase 1) enzyme. It is used in the treatment of certain hematologic malignancies, particularly acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with an IDH1 mutation. By inhibiting the mutated enzyme, ivosidenib helps restore normal cellular metabolism, reducing the production of an oncometabolite called 2-hydroxyglutarate, which is associated with cancer progression. Ivosidenib is approved for use in patients with relapsed or refractory AML and other IDH1-mutant cancers.
The developed model is PK 2 compartments model.

The disposition of ivosidenib in the target population, identification of intrinsic and extrinsic factors influencing ivosidenib PK variability (including concomitant medications: Cyp inhibitors).

- Time scale: days to weeks
- Biological scale: Whole body

description: A systems model was developed to characterize the metabolism and disposition of ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) and its conjugates in healthy individuals, using pharmacokinetic (PK) data from published studies. This model was designed to investigate the distribution of oral UDCA and explore potential interactions affecting its therapeutic effects when its enterohepatic recirculation is disrupted. The base model was empirically adapted for patients with primary biliary cirrhosis (PBC), incorporating current knowledge of disease pathophysiology and clinical data. Simulations were performed for PBC patients under two competing hypotheses: one where both UDCA and its conjugates' ileal absorption is inhibited, and another where only the conjugates' absorption is inhibited. The simulations revealed distinct differences in bile acid distribution patterns in plasma and bile. The adapted UDCA model for PBC patients offers a platform to study complex therapeutic drug interactions involving UDCA, its conjugates, and the inhibition of ileal bile acid transport in this rare patient population.

description:
Patritumab Deruxtecan (HER3-DXd) is an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) targeting the HER3 receptor (a member of the EGFR family), which is overexpressed in various cancers. The drug consists of a fully human monoclonal antibody (patritumab) linked to a topoisomerase I inhibitor (deruxtecan), which is a cytotoxic payload. Upon binding to HER3 on cancer cells, the ADC is internalized, and the cytotoxic agent is released, leading to cell death.
Population PK modeling for deruxtecan (DXd)-conjugated antibody (representing patritumab deruxtecan) and unconjugated MAAA-1181a (DXd, payload) was carried out sequentially. DXd-conjugated antibody PK was described using a 2-compartment model with parallel linear and nonlinear clearance. Unconjugated DXd PK was described using a 1-compartment model with linear clearance and release of DXd as a first-order, time-dependent function of the level of DXd-conjugated antibody in the central compartment.

description:
Glasdegib is a small molecule inhibitor of the Hedgehog signaling pathway, specifically targeting the Smoothened (SMO) receptor. It is used in the treatment of certain types of cancer, particularly acute myeloid leukemia (AML). By inhibiting the Hedgehog pathway, Glasdegib can help to prevent the proliferation and survival of cancer cells. It is typically used in combination with low-dose cytarabine for patients with newly diagnosed AML who are not candidates for intensive chemotherapy.
A 2-compartment model best described the data and included the following parameters: apparent total clearance (CL/F), apparent central volume of distribution (Vc/F), apparent peripheral volume of distribution (Vp/F), intercompartmental clearance (Q/F), and first-order absorption rate constant (ka). Interindividual variability (IIV) in the PK parameters was modeled using multiplicative exponential random effects. Residual variability was modeled using a proportional error model with log-transformed glasdegib plasma concentrations and thetarized sigma.

description:
Sunitinib is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor used in the treatment of certain types of cancer. It works by blocking multiple molecular targets involved in tumor growth, angiogenesis (the formation of new blood vessels), and metastatic progression of cancer. Sunitinib is commonly prescribed for renal cell carcinoma (kidney cancer), gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST), and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. It interferes with the pathways that promote cancer cell proliferation and blood supply, thereby inhibiting tumor growth and spread.
N-desethyl sunitinib (SU12662), the active metabolite of sunitinib, shows similar activity to sunitinib.
The developed model is a one and two-compartments pharmacokinetic (PK) model describing the distribution and elimination of a sunitinib and SU12662 respectively. The first compartment typically represents the central compartment, which includes the bloodstream and tissues where the drug is rapidly distributed. The second compartment represents peripheral tissues, where the drug distribution is slower.
It was assumed that the sunitinib concentration in the hypothetical enzyme site was in equilibrium with the plasma concentration in the sunitinib central compartment. Sunitinib is firstly absorbed to the hypothetical enzyme compartment, where sunitinib remains either unchanged or is metabolized. Subsequently, unchanged sunitinib and metabolite distribute to the central compartment. Sunitinib is further metabolized from the systemic circulation by the same enzyme. SU12662 distributes to its peripheral compartment and can also be metabolized from the systemic circulation.
contextOfUse:
- Treatment: Sunitinib and its active metabolite SU12662
- Species: Human
- Pathology: Cancer
- Biological context: The primary aims of the model were to develop an improved PK model that circumvented some previous model limitations (e.g. limited dataset or lacked important elements such as correlations between sunitinib and its metabolite) and to prove the utility of the PK model in treatment optimization in clinical practice.
- Virtual population characteristics: Introduce variability using allometric scaling for clearance and volume of distribution for both sunitinib and SU12662. Inter-Individual Variability has been also introduced on clearance; volume of distribution of the central compartment. Some correlation has been introduced on both sunitinib and SU12662 clearance, both volume of distribution of the central compartment , and between clearance and volume of distribution of the central compartment  of SU12662 as 0.53, 0.48 and 0.45, respectively. description:
Fms-related tyrosine kinase 3 ligand (FLT3L) is a soluble growth factor (or cytokine) that plays a critical role in regulating hematopoiesis. FLT3L functions by agonizing the fms-related tyrosine kinase 3 receptor (FLT3; also known as CD135), which is expressed on dentritic cells (DC) as well as myeloid and lymphoid progenitor cells. Agonism of this pathway initiates signaling cascades that promote DC proliferation, differentiation, mobilization, and survival. FLT3L treatment has been shown to expand not only cDC1s, but also conventional type 2 DCs (cDC2s), plasmacytoid DCs (pDCs), and other immune cells of myeloid origin in blood, lymphoid organs, and tumors.
The FLT3L-Fc minimal PBPK model is a second-generation mPBPK model specifically for monoclonal antibodies (mAb) by including (lumping) several essential components of mAb PK used in full PBPK models. These components include convection as the primary mechanism of antibody movement from plasma into tissues and return to plasma with interstitial fluid as the major extravascular distribution space. Tight tissue represents  muscle, skin, adipose and brain whereas leaky tissue represent all other tissues (liver, kidney, heart, etc.).
A one-compartment model with a subcutaneous compartment and a nonlinear clearance term describes the PK of FLT3L (CDX-301).Free drug in the central compartment is linked to an indirect PD response model via a series of transit compartments to capture the observed expansion of cDC1 and cDC2 cells.

- Species: Human. Note the initial model was also used for cynomolgus monkeys, but for now only human parameter set is implemented.

- Biological context: This model has been developed to integrate the molecule’s mechanism of action, as well as the complex preclinical and clinical PK/PD data, to support the preclinical-to-clinical translation of FLT3L-Fc.

description:
Vincristine (VCR) is a chemotherapy medication used to treat various types of cancer, including leukemia, lymphoma, and solid tumors. It belongs to a class of drugs called vinca alkaloids, which work by inhibiting the growth of cancer cells by preventing their division. Vincristine is usually administered intravenously and is often part of a combination chemotherapy regimen.
The developed model is a two-compartment pharmacokinetic (PK) model describing the distribution and elimination of a drug within the body using two interconnected compartments. The first compartment typically represents the central compartment, which includes the bloodstream and tissues where the drug is rapidly distributed. The second compartment represents peripheral tissues, where the drug distribution is slower.
A  tumor compartment has also been introduced but fluxes between central and tumor are kept to 0 for now.

- Species: Human (pediatric)
- Pathology: Cancer
- Biological context: The primary aims of the model were: (1) to assess PK of VCR given to pediatric cancer patients as either push injections or 1 h infusions; (2) to investigate the association between PK and exposure parameters and induced peripheral neuropathy ; (3) to investigate the effect of concurrent azole antifungal treatment on VCR PK parameters.
- Virtual population characteristics: Introduce variability using covariates (body mass and body surface area). Inter-Individual Variability has been also introduced on clearance; volume of distribution of the central compartment; inter-compartmental clearance and volume of distribution of the peripheral compartment.

description:
Venetoclax, sold under brand names Venclexta and Venclyxto, is a medication used to treat specific types of adult cancers. It targets chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), as well as newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML) in certain patients. It works by blocking a protein called BCL-2. BCL-2 is part of a family of proteins known as the BCL-2 family, which play a crucial role in regulating cell death, or apoptosis. BCL-2 acts as an anti-apoptotic protein, meaning it helps cells resist cell death signals. In cancer cells, BCL-2 can be over-expressed, allowing them to survive and proliferate uncontrollably. Venetoclax acts as a BH3 mimetic, mimicking the structure of a specific group of proteins within the BCL-2 family called BH3-only proteins.
These BH3-only proteins naturally bind to BCL-2, neutralizing its anti-apoptotic function and triggering cell death. Venetoclax binds to the same pocket on BCL-2 as BH3-only proteins, effectively blocking their binding and allowing them to activate cell death pathways. This ultimately leads to the programmed death of cancer cells.
Venetoclax is administered orally and the pharmacokinetic is described a 3 compartments PK model

description: Monomethyl auristatin E (MMAE)-based Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs) are a type of cancer therapy. They combine a specific antibody that targets cancer cells with a potent cytotoxic drug called MMAE. When the ADC binds to cancer cells, it releases MMAE inside them, causing cell death. MMAE works by blocking cell division, which is particularly effective against rapidly dividing cancer cells.The model characterizes different ADC analytes of MMAE-based ADCs, including ADC, conjugated MMAE, unconjugated MMAE and naked mAb. Each ADC analyte is assumed to be described using a 2-compartmental PK model (parameterized in terms of CL, CLD, V1 and V2), cf figure below.

description:
Lapatinib is a targeted therapy drug used for patients with advanced or metastatic breast cancer, particularly for those whose tumors overexpress the HER2 protein and have not responded well to other treatments.. It is specifically designed to inhibit the tyrosine kinase activity associated with two receptors: the Human Epidermal growth factor Receptor 2 (HER2) and the Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR). Both of these receptors are involved in the pathways that lead to cell growth and proliferation. Overexpression of HER2 is found in a significant percentage of breast cancers and is associated with aggressive disease and poor prognosis.
The PBPK model for lapatinib was developed incorporating absorption, intestinal and hepatic metabolism and fecal elimination. This flow-limited model was comprised of eight tissue compartments: plasma, brain, heart, lung, kidney, intestine, liver and slowly perfused tissues (remaining tissues).

description:
cibisatamab (also known as RG7802 or RO6958688) is an investigational bispecific monoclonal antibody designed for cancer immunotherapy. Cibisatamab targets both CD3 on T cells and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) on tumor cells. By binding to these two different molecules simultaneously, cibisatamab brings T cells into close proximity with cancer cells, which is intended to promote the activation of the T cells and direct their cytotoxic (cell-killing) activity specifically towards the cancer cells expressing CEA.
This mechanism of action represents a type of immunotherapy approach called T-cell engaging bispecific antibody therapy. CEA is a protein that is overexpressed in several types of cancers, including colorectal cancer and some other gastrointestinal cancers, making it a promising target for therapies designed to harness the immune system to fight cancer.
Pharmacokinetics of Cibisatamab were described by a a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (mPBPK) model. The quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model consists of four compartments: central, peripheral, tumour and tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN). Central and peripheral compartments represent the total volume of blood and peripheral tissues, respectively. Tumour compartment represents the total tumour volume, which is assumed to be constant for the purpose of antibody pharmacokinetics and effector T-cell transport. TDLN compartment represents a lumped lymph node assuming that the antibody will be evenly distributed among multiple TDLNs that have the same antibody and T-cell dynamics.

- Biological context: The model was developed to conduct in silico virtual clinical trials for combination therapy of a PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor (atezolizumab) and a bispecific T cell engager (cibisatamab). The model compare various doses and schedules of administration for two drugs with the goal of therapy optimization. Moreover, the model was developed to quantify the score of drug synergy for these two drugs to further study the role of the combination therapy.

description:
Carboplatin is a chemotherapy medication used to treat various forms of cancer, including ovarian cancer, lung cancer, head and neck cancer, and more. It belongs to a class of drugs known as platinum-containing compounds, which also includes cisplatin and oxaliplatin. These drugs work by interfering with the DNA of cancer cells, preventing them from replicating and causing them to die, thereby inhibiting tumor growth.
The developed model is a two-compartment pharmacokinetic (PK) model describes the distribution and elimination of a drug within the body using two interconnected compartments. The first compartment typically represents the central compartment, which includes the bloodstream and tissues where the drug is rapidly distributed. The second compartment represents peripheral tissues, where the drug distribution is slower.

description:
Mepolizumab is a IgG1 kappa monoclonal antibody used as a medication to treat certain severe forms of asthma and other conditions associated with eosinophilic inflammation. It specifically targets interleukin-5 (IL-5), a cytokine involved in the growth, activation, and survival of eosinophils, which are a type of white blood cell that plays a key role in the inflammatory process of asthma and certain other diseases.
This minimal PBPK model is a second-generation mPBPK model specifically for monoclonal antibodies (mAb) by including (lumping) several essential components of mAb PK used in full PBPK models. These components include convection as the primary mechanism of antibody movement from plasma into tissues and return to plasma with interstitial fluid as the major extravascular distribution space. Tight tissue represents  muscle, skin, adipose and brain whereas leaky tissue represent all other tissues (liver, kidney, heart, etc.).

description:
Entinostat is a histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor. Entinostat induces a shift of cell cycle from S phase to G1 phase. This can lead to cancer cell death or prevent them from multiplying.
Pharmacokinetics of Entinostat were described by a a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (mPBPK) model. The quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model consists of 6 compartments: buccal, gastrointestinal tract, central, peripheral, tumour and tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN). Central and peripheral compartments represent the total volume of blood and peripheral tissues, respectively. Tumour compartment represents the total tumour volume, which is assumed to be constant for the purpose of antibody pharmacokinetics and effector T-cell transport. TDLN compartment represents a lumped lymph node assuming that the antibody will be evenly distributed among multiple TDLNs that have the same antibody and T-cell dynamics.

description: Ezetimibe is an oral medication used to treat high blood cholesterol and certain other lipid abnormalities. Ezetimibe has been approved since 2002 (USA) and several studies have been performed since then to assess its PK and efficacy as monotherapy or combined with other atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) treatments (eg. statins) which ensures the availability of data in literature.Based on ordinary differential equations, the PK model focuses on the study of drug concentration changes over time in the body. It examines how drugs are absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and eliminated. It does not explicitly consider specific organs but it provides an overall understanding of drug behavior in the body.The applicability domain (AD) of thisorgan-levelsubmodel is the description of the pharmacokinetics (PK) for adaily dose of 10mg p.o.of Ezetimibe drug after oral administration in adults ASCVD patients suffering hyperlipidemia.
contextOfUse:
- Treatment  : Ezetimibe
- Species:  Human
- Pathology  : Hyperlipidemia
- Biological context  : The Ezetimibe model simulates the pharmacokinetics of the Ezetimibe drug (PK) and accounts for the drug administration, distribution and elimination for a daily dose of 10mg p.o. for adult patients. Enterohepatic recirculation is not modeled as enterohepatic recirculation of Ezetimibe is negligible when considering multiple daily administrations.

description:
Nifedipine (3,5-dimethyl 2,6-dimethyl-4-(2-nitrophenyl)-1,4-dihydropyridine-3,5-dicarboxylate) is a calcium channel blocker medication used to manage angina, high blood pressure, Raynaud's phenomenon, and premature labor.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics. A PBPK model including 15 tissue compartments was implemented. The body for this model was composed of the following tissues: lung, adipose, bone, brain, heart, kidney, muscle, skin, liver, pancreas, spleen, and gut. The model also included compartments for venous blood and arterial blood, and a “rest-of-body” compartment that represented the remainder of the tissues not explicitly included in the model. The volume of the rest-of-body compartment was derived by subtracting the volumes of all other compartments from body weight, whereas the blood flow to this compartment was derived by subtracting the blood flows of all compartments entering into venous blood from the cardiac output.

projectDescription: Darta Maija Zake, Janis Kurlovics, Linda Zaharenko, Vitalijs Komasilovs, Janis Klovins, Egils Stalidzans -- BIOMD0000001028
description:
Metformin is a medication for the treatment of type 2 diabetes.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The remainder, heart, fat, muscle, brain, lungs, stomach, liver, portal vein, venous and arterial plasma, and red blood cells are defined as independent one compartment organs, while kidney and small intestine were developed as multi-compartment organs. The concentrations in other organs such as the bone, skin, colon, etc., were not considered as separate compartments, and they are united in the model as a remainder compartment. An additional red blood cells (RBC) compartment was implemented in the human model structure due to the fact that the concentration time-course data in red blood cells was available in humans while it was missing for mice.
The model is developed assuming that all compartments except for the kidney and the small intestine are well stirred. It is assumed that the drug distribution into these compartments is driven by permeability-limited kinetics. The assumption of permeability-limited kinetics is based on the hydrophilic characteristics of metformin. Active transport component is only applied in the compartments of the kidney and small intestine.

description:
Pravastatin is an anionic, hepatoselective, hydrophilic statin which reduces total cholesterol, Low-density Lipoprotein Cholesterol (LDL-C) and triglyceride levels, and increases High-density Lipoprotein Cholesterol (HDL-C) in patients with hypercholesterolemia.
The developed PBPK model of pravastatin was applied to study the differences in pharmacokinetics between healthy subjects and patients with either hepatic or renal impairment, as well as the differences between patients with either the wildtype or a specific genetic variant of the enzymatic transporters (OATP2B1, OATP1B1 and MRP2).
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics except for liver where the transpor is actively carried out by Organic Anionic Transport Polypeptide 1B1, (OATP1B1) and export from the liver in the bile via Multidrug Resistance-Associated Protein 2 (MRP2).
Whole body model consisting of liver, kidney, intestine, lung and the remaining organs are not modeled explicitly and lumped into the rest compartment. No tissue distribution exists for pravastatin besides the active transport in the liver.
Pravastatin can be administered intravenously (IV) or orally (PO) with Pravastatin appearing in the venous blood or intestine, respectively.
Metabolisation of pravastatin in the liver is neglected.
Pravastatin is eliminated from the body via the urine and the faeces.

description:
Tremelimumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody that blocks the CTLA-4 protein. CTLA-4 is a protein that helps to regulate the immune system by preventing T cells from becoming activated. By blocking CTLA-4, tremelimumab helps to boost the immune system and make it more effective at fighting cancer.
Tremelimumab is approved by the FDA for the treatment of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), a type of liver cancer, in combination with another drug called durvalumab. It is also approved for the treatment of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) in combination with durvalumab and platinum-based chemotherapy in patients who have not been treated before.
Pharmacokinetics of Tremelimumab were described by a a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (mPBPK) model. The quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model consists of four compartments: central, peripheral, tumour and tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN). Central and peripheral compartments represent the total volume of blood and peripheral tissues, respectively. Tumour compartment represents the total tumour volume, which is assumed to be constant for the purpose of antibody pharmacokinetics and effector T-cell transport. TDLN compartment represents a lumped lymph node assuming that the antibody will be evenly distributed among multiple TDLNs that have the same antibody and T-cell dynamics.

description:
Simvastatin (SV) is a a nonpolar and highly lipophilic statin. Simvastatin is a prodrug that must be activated in the liver by esterases to the main active metabolite, simvastatin acid (SVA) which reduces total cholesterol, Low-density Lipoprotein Cholesterol (LDL-C) and triglyceride levels, and increases High-density Lipoprotein Cholesterol (HDL-C) in patients with hypercholesterolemia. Both SV and SVA are then metabolized into less active metabolites (SVM).
The PBPK/PD model developed to predict simvastatin pharmacokineticsand cholesterol pharmacodynamics) was applied to study LDL-C lowering therapy with simvastatin in different familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) classes.
The PBPK/PD model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The whole-body model consists of the liver, kidney, gastrointestinal tract, lungs, and the rest compartment.
- In the liver compartment , SV is converted into SVM by CYP3A. Alternatively, esterases catalyze the reaction of SV to SVA with subsequent conversion to SVM by CYP3A4. This is the main activation process of simvastatin. SV, SVA, and SVM can be exchanged between the liver and the circulation. SVM accounts for all simvastatin metabolites after metabolism by either CYP3A4 or esterases. SVM can be transported via enterohepatic circulation from the liver into the gastrointestinal tract.
- The  inte  stinal compartment describes the dissolution and absorption of SV, absorption of SV, and first pass metabolism of SV to SVM via CYP3A4 in the enterocytes of the intestinal wall. Only a fraction of SVM that reaches the intestine via the enterohepatic circulation is absorbed, with the remainder excreted in the feces, whereas SV can be completely absorbed.
- The kidney model describes the urinary excretion of SVM. There is no renal clearance of SV and SVA.
The model was extended to include the major processes affecting plasma LDL-C levels. The liver model includes the major processes relevant to hepatic cholesterol homeostasis, including dietary cholesterol uptake, fecal cholesterol loss, a shortened cholesterol biosynthetic pathway, and uptake of cholesterol from plasma as LDL-C. Cholesterol can be exported from the liver as VLDL-C. Hepatic cholesterol synthesis is modeled via the precursor reaction from acetyl-CoA to HMG-CoA mediated by HMG-CoA synthase and the reaction from HMG-CoA to cholesterol mediated by HMG-CoA reductase. SVA and SVM are competitive inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase. An important regulatory mechanism in the model is the adjustment of protein levels of HMG-CoA synthase, HMG-CoA reductase and LDLR by hepatic cholesterol levels. As cholesterol levels decrease, protein synthesis rates increase and protein degradation rates decrease for these proteins, resulting in an increase in these key enzymes of cholesterol synthesis.
A key component of the model is the hepatic LDL receptor (LDLR) pathway consisting of (1) LDLR synthesis; (2) transport of LDLR to the membrane; (3) binding of LDL-C to LDLR; (4) internalization of the LDLR-LDL-C complex; (5) recycling of LDLR; and (P) degradation of LDLR.

description:
Durvalumab (brand name Imfinzi) is a fully human monoclonal antibody that blocks the programmed cell death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) protein. PD-L1 is a protein that helps cancer cells evade the immune system. By blocking PD-L1, durvalumab helps the immune system to recognize and attack cancer cells.
Durvalumab is approved by the FDA for the treatment of a variety of cancers, including: Urothelial carcinoma Unresectable, Stage III non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), Biliary tract cancer, Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), Esophageal and gastric cancer, Small cell lung cancer (SCLC), Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC).
Pharmacokinetics of Durvalumab were described by a a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (mPBPK) model. The quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model consists of four compartments: central, peripheral, tumour and tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN). Central and peripheral compartments represent the total volume of blood and peripheral tissues, respectively. Tumour compartment represents the total tumour volume, which is assumed to be constant for the purpose of antibody pharmacokinetics and effector T-cell transport. TDLN compartment represents a lumped lymph node assuming that the antibody will be evenly distributed among multiple TDLNs that have the same antibody and T-cell dynamics.

description:
Paracetamol (acetaminophen[a] or para-hydroxyacetanilide) is a non-opioid analgesic and antipyretic agent used to treat fever and mild to moderate pain.
The developed model use ordinary differential equations.
The whole-body PBPK module describes the absorption, distribution and clearance of acetaminophen (APAP). APAP is absorbed from GI tract, distributed throughout the body via the systematic circulation, and excreted by the kidneys. The liver receives both arterial and venous blood.

description:
Dextromethorphan (DXM) is an over-the-counter, antitussive, non-narcotic, synthetic analog of codeine affecting the activity of numerous channels and receptors in the brain that trigger the cough reflex.
The PBPK model was applied to investigate the genotype-phenotype association and the role of CYP2D6 polymorphisms for metabolic phenotyping using the urinary cumulative metabolic ratio (UCMR), defined as $ \frac{Dextromethorphan (DXM)}{ (Active metabolite dextrorphan (DXO) +  Dextorphan glucuronide (DXO-Glu))}  $
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics to describe Dextromethorphan, active metabolite dextrorphan (DXO) and Dextorphan glucuronide (DXO-Glu) dynamics.
Whole body model consisting of liver, kidney, intestine, forearm, lung and the remaining organs are not modeled explicitly and lumped into the rest compartment.
DXM can be administered intravenously (IV) or orally (PO) with DXM appearing in the venous blood or intestine, respectively.
Metabolic reactions take place in the intestine and liver. The conversion of DXM to DXO can be either catalyzed via CYP2D6 (main route) or CYP3A4 (minor route) in the liver. N-demethylation of DXM in the liver is neglected.
Kidney model for the urinary excretion of DXM, DXO, and DXO-Glu.

- Virtual population characteristics: model was also capable to predict UCMR distributions for different biogeographical populations: Oceanian, Near Eastern, American, Latino, Central/South Asian, African American/Afro-Caribbean, Sub-Saharan African, European, and East Asian populations.
- Time scale: Hour

description:
Remdesivir is a prodrug that is intended to allow intracellular delivery of GS-441524 monophosphate and subsequent biotransformation into GS-441524 triphosphate, a ribonucleotide analogue inhibitor of viral RNA polymerase.
A two-compartment PK model was used to convert remdesivir intravenous (i.v.) dose to plasma concentration. By assuming a drug free plasma concentration of remdesivir in equilibrium with the tissue (extracellular) concentration, the compartmental PK model is linked to the intracellular metabolism model, which describes the diffusion of remdesivir across the cell membrane and conversion of the parent drug remdesivir to its active metabolite nucleoside TP inside the cell.

description:
Atezolizumab is a fully humanized monoclonal antibody that blocks the programmed cell death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) protein. PD-L1 is a protein that helps cancer cells evade the immune system. By blocking PD-L1, atezolizumab helps the immune system to recognize and attack cancer cells.
Atezolizumab is approved by the FDA for the treatment of a variety of cancers, including:
- Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC)  , either first-line (with bevacizumab) or second-line or later
- Small cell lung cancer (SCLC)  , in combination with carboplatin-etoposide
- Urothelial carcinoma (UC)  , in combination with platinum-based chemotherapy
- Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)  , in combination with carboplatin-bevacizumab
- Metastatic melanoma  , in patients with PD-L1-positive tumors
- Advanced alveolar soft part sarcoma (ASPS)
Pharmacokinetics of Atezolizumab were described by a a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (mPBPK) model. The quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model consists of four compartments: central, peripheral, tumour and tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN). Central and peripheral compartments represent the total volume of blood and peripheral tissues, respectively. Tumour compartment represents the total tumour volume, which is assumed to be constant for the purpose of antibody pharmacokinetics and effector T-cell transport. TDLN compartment represents a lumped lymph node assuming that the antibody will be evenly distributed among multiple TDLNs that have the same antibody and T-cell dynamics.

description:
Trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1) is an antibody-drug conjugate consisting of the humanized monoclonal antibody trastuzumab (Herceptin) covalently linked to the cytotoxic agent DM1. Trastuzumab alone stops growth of cancer cells by binding to the HER2 receptor, whereas trastuzumab emtansine undergoes receptor-mediated internalization into cells, is catabolized in lysosomes where DM1-containing catabolites are released and subsequently bind tubulin to cause mitotic arrest and cell death.
The developed model is composed of three integrated submodels for ADCs, that is parameterized with: (i) human pharmacokinetics (PKs) using a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) model, (ii) spatial distribution of ADCs across the tumor using a Krogh cylinder model, and (iii) a tumor growth inhibition model.

description:
Ipilimumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody that blocks the cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4 (CTLA-4) receptor. CTLA-4 is a protein that helps to regulate the immune system by preventing T cells from becoming activated. By blocking CTLA-4, ipilimumab helps to boost the immune system and make it more effective at fighting cancer.
Ipilimumab is approved by the FDA for the treatment of advanced melanoma, non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), and metastatic renal cell carcinoma (RCC). It is also being studied for the treatment of other types of cancer, such as bladder cancer, pancreatic cancer, and colorectal cancer.
Pharmacokinetics of Ipilimumab were described by a a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (mPBPK) model. The quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model consists of four compartments: central, peripheral, tumour and tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN). Central and peripheral compartments represent the total volume of blood and peripheral tissues, respectively. Tumour compartment represents the total tumour volume, which is assumed to be constant for the purpose of antibody pharmacokinetics and effector T-cell transport. TDLN compartment represents a lumped lymph node assuming that the antibody will be evenly distributed among multiple TDLNs that have the same antibody and T-cell dynamics.

description:
Artemether is a medication used for the treatment of malaria.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics. A PBPK model including 15 tissue compartments was implemented. The body for this model was composed of the following tissues: lung, adipose, bone, brain, heart, kidney, muscle, skin, liver, pancreas, spleen, and gut. The model also included compartments for venous blood and arterial blood, and a “rest-of-body” compartment that represented the remainder of the tissues not explicitly included in the model. The volume of the rest-of-body compartment was derived by subtracting the volumes of all other compartments from body weight, whereas the blood flow to this compartment was derived by subtracting the blood flows of all compartments entering into venous blood from the cardiac output.

- Virtual population characteristics: The models use a body weight of 60 and 73 kg, total cardiac output of 5.9 and 6.5 L/min, tissue volume and blood flow parameters for females and males

- Biological scale: Whole body

description:
Chlorzoxazone is a muscle relaxant used to treat muscle spasms and low back pain. Its primary metabolism occurs in the liver via CYP2E1-mediated 6-hydroxylation.
The PBPK model was applied to investigate how changes in CYP2E1 level and activity, ethanol consumption and abstinence, and liver impairment might affect the results of metabolic phenotyping with chlorzoxazone.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system.
The PBPK model is hierarchically organized (see Fig just below) and allows simulation of the time courses of chlorzoxazone, 6-hydroxychlorzoxazone, chlorzoxazone-O-glucuronide and ethanol. The top layer represents the whole body and systemic circulation coupled with organ models for lung, liver, kidney, intestine, and the rest compartment and transport via the systemic circulation.
The intestine model describes the dissolution, absorption, and excretion of chlorzoxazone. Only a fraction of the administered chlorzoxazone dose is absorbed into the systemic circulation, with the remainder being excreted in the feces.
In the liver, chlorzoxazone is metabolized to 6-hydroxychlorzoxazone via CYP2E1 and subsequently converted to chlorzoxazone-O-glucuronide.
The kidney model describes the renal excretion of chlorzoxazone-O-glucuronide into the urine.
Ethanol is absorbed into the systemic circulation in the intestine and eliminated in the liver. Ethanol affects chlorzoxazone metabolism by inhibiting the degradation of CYP2E1 in the liver, resulting in increased CYP2E1 and conversion of chlorzoxazone.

description:
Midazolam  is one of the most effective and safe medicines used for anesthesia and procedural sedation, as well as treating trouble sleeping and severe agitation.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics for all organs except liver that use permeability rate-limited kinetics.
Whole body model consisting of gut, kidney, liver, lung, spleen, pancreas and the remaining organs are not modeled explicitly and lumped into the rest compartment.
Midazolam can be administered intravenously (IV) or orally (PO) with Midazolam appearing in the venous blood or intestine, respectively.
Midazolam is metabolised in the liver as well as in the small intestine by CYP3A4. The PBPK model does not include the secondary metabolic route of midazolam,i.e.the conversion of midazolam to 4-hydroxymidazolam.
Kidney model for the urinary excretion of 1-hydroxymidazolam.

description:
Indocyanine green (ICG) is an inert, anionic, water-soluble, tricarbocyanine dye that is bound to plasma proteins. After intravenous administration ICG is taken up exclusively by the liver and excreted unchanged into the bile. It is not reabsorbed by the intestine and does not undergo enterohepatic circulation. ICG is an ideal test compound to test hepatic uptake and biliary excretion.
The PBPK model was applied (i) to study the effect of liver cirrhosis and liver resection on ICG pharmacokinetics; and (ii) to evaluate the model-based prediction of postoperative ICG-R15 (retention ratio 15 min after administration) as a measure for postoperative outcome.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics for all organs except liver that use permeability rate-limited kinetics.
To simulate the whole-body distribution and hepatic elimination of ICG two models were coupled: (i) A whole-body model describing the distribution of ICG in the body and to the organs via blood flow. (ii) A liver model which describes hepatic uptake of ICG, biliary excretion of ICG and transport of ICG into the feces. ICG pharmacokinetic parameters (i.e., ICG-PDR, R15, clearance, half-life) were calculated from the resulting time course predictions of ICG in the venous plasma.
The model focuses on the accurate description of the first 15–30 min of ICG elimination relevant for the calculation of the ICG parameters, which is well-described by a monophasic disappearance pattern. In reality, a biphasic disappearance pattern of ICG with a second, slower phase of plasma elimination can be observed.
ICG can be administered intravenously (IV) with ICG appearing in the venous blood.

description:
Atorvastatin (AS) is a synthetic lipid-lowering agent. Atorvastatin competitively inhibits hepatic hydroxymethyl-glutaryl coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) reductase, the enzyme which catalyzes the conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate, a key step in cholesterol synthesis. Atorvastatin also increases the number of LDL receptors on hepatic cell surfaces to enhance uptake and catabolism of LDL and reduces LDL production and the number of LDL particles. This agent lowers plasma cholesterol and lipoprotein levels.
The model describes a deterministic modeling approach of the dynamic biotransformation and transport processes of AS in human hepatocytes considering both kinetics of transport processes as well as intracellular detoxification processes via Phase I and Phase II enzymes.
Attention is given to the detailed enzymatic and chemical reactions, including conversions between the acid and lactone form of AS as well as unspecific binding between metabolites and macromolecules like proteins.
The model simulatein vitroexposure of primary human hepatocytes to Atorvastatin.

description:
Alfentanil is a potent but short-acting synthetic opioid analgesic drug, used for anesthesia in surgery.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics. A PBPK model including 15 tissue compartments was implemented. The body for this model was composed of the following tissues: lung, adipose, bone, brain, heart, kidney, muscle, skin, liver, pancreas, spleen, and gut. The model also included compartments for venous blood and arterial blood, and a “rest-of-body” compartment that represented the remainder of the tissues not explicitly included in the model. The volume of the rest-of-body compartment was derived by subtracting the volumes of all other compartments from body weight, whereas the blood flow to this compartment was derived by subtracting the blood flows of all compartments entering into venous blood from the cardiac output.

description:
Pembrolizumab is a cancer immunotherapy drug that works by blocking a protein called programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1). PD-1 is a protein that helps cancer cells evade the immune system. By blocking PD-1, Pembrolizumab helps the immune system to recognize and attack cancer cells.
Pembrolizumab is used to treat a variety of cancers, including melanoma, lung cancer, kidney cancer, and head and neck cancer. It is also used to treat some types of blood cancer, such as Hodgkin lymphoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
Pharmacokinetics of Pembrolizumab were described by a a minimal physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (mPBPK) model. The quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model consists of four compartments: central, peripheral, tumour and tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN). Central and peripheral compartments represent the total volume of blood and peripheral tissues, respectively. Tumour compartment represents the total tumour volume, which is assumed to be constant for the purpose of antibody pharmacokinetics and effector T-cell transport. TDLN compartment represents a lumped lymph node assuming that the antibody will be evenly distributed among multiple TDLNs that have the same antibody and T-cell dynamics.

description:
Omeprazole is a proton-pump inhibitor (PPI) used in the treatment of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), peptic ulcer disease, and Zollinger–Ellison syndrome.
The PBPK model was was developed to investigate the pharmacokinetic (omeprazole time courses) /pharmacodynamic (changes in stomach pH and gastric acid output) aspects of omeprazole.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The whole-body model allows to simulate the absorption and distribution of omeprazole as well as the distribution of omeprazole metabolites. The whole-body model was coupled to tissue models of the liver, kidney, and stomach.
The liver submodel describes the uptake of omeprazole from the plasma, the metabolic conversion to inactive omeprazole metabolites by CYP2C19 andCYP3A4, and the export of the omeprazole metabolites back in the plasma.
The kidney model the renal excretion of omeprazole metabolites.
The stomach model describes the activity of proton pumps (H+/ K+ATPase) and the turnover of the proton-pump (PP) via synthesis and degradation. PP can be irreversibly inhibited via omeprazole. The following assumptions were made: (i) degradation of the active and inhibited PP is identical; (ii) only the active PP can secrete protons in the gastric fluid; (iii) the gastric acid (with protons) is transported in the stomach via gastric acid secretion and changes the stomach pH; (iv) gastric acid can be lost from the stomach to the intestine.

description:
Osimertinib is a targeted therapy used in the context of non-small-cell lung carcinomas. It is an oral, third-generation, irreversible EGFR-TKI that selectively inhibits both EGFR-TKI sensitizing and EGFR T790M resistance mutations. Thus, It is indicated for the treatment of adults with locally advanced or metastatic EGFR T790M mutation-positive NSCLC; also for first-line treatment of patients with locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC with activating EGFR mutations. It is the only third-generation EGFR-TKI approved by major regulatory agencies.The main metabolic pathways of osimertinib were oxidation and dealkylation. Two pharmacologically active metabolites (AZ7550 and AZ5104) have been identified in the plasma after oral dosing with osimertinib. Although AZ7550 showed a similar pharmacological profile to osimertinib while AZ5104 showed greater potency across both mutant and wild-type EGFR. Moreover In vitro cell potency in EGFR mutation and EGFR-wild type suggests AZ5104 may contribute to safety and efficacy, whilst AZ7550 is unlikely to.Based on ordinary differential equations, the PBPK model focuses on the relationship between the concentration of a drug in the body (pharmacokinetics) and its pharmacological effects (pharmacodynamics). It quantifies how drug concentrations at different sites, including the liver, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, and lungs -considered as models-, influence the desired therapeutic effects or adverse reactions. It does not explicitly consider specific organs but it provides an overall understanding of drug behavior in the body. It considers factors such as drug-receptor interactions, drug metabolism, and the time course of drug effects.The applicability domain of the osimertinib model is to predict the drug concentrations in the plasma and majors organs with daily dose of 80 mg.
This model accounts for the administration, distribution, metabolism and elimination of osimertinib both in humans and in mice as shown in Figure 1.

- Virtual population characteristics  : The models use a body weight of 60 and 73 kg, total cardiac output of 5.9 and 6.5 L/min, tissue volume and blood flow parameters for females and males above 30 years old
- Time scale  : Hour
- Biological scale  : Whole body

description:
PEG-IFN$\alpha$-2a (pegylated with a branched 40kDa PEG chain) is an antiviral drug which has a dual mode of action including both antiviral and immunomodulatory effects. It is approved around the world, such as in the EU, the US, China and many other countries, on the treatment of chronic hepatitis B (CHB).
Based on ordinary differential equations, thisPBPKmodel focuses on the relationship between the drug's physicochemical properties and its distribution in the body (pharmacokinetics). It quantifies the drug concentration in the main tissues of the organism, including the liver, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, and lungs -considered as models-, based on administration regimen as well as virtual patient physiology. It does not explicitly consider treatment pharmacological effects (pharmacodynamics), but could be combined with such efficacy model. It considers factors such as drug-receptor interactions, drug metabolism, and clearance.
IFN is a therapeutic protein and pysiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) approaches have demonstrated utility in predicting the time-course of therapeutic proteins when administered intravenously . It is however administered subcutaneously and in order to model this accordingly, the model was developed based on the work of Offman et al. in which a PBPK is developed for.
This model accounts for the administration, distribution and elimination, metabolism and excretion of PegIfn2a both in humans and in mice.

- Treatment  : PegIfn2a
- Species:  Human, Mouse
- Pathology  : Hepatitis B (HBV) which is a viral infection that attacks the liver and causes both acute and chronic diseases.
- Biological context  : The PegIfn2a model was developed to investigate the impact of PegIfn2a on two major endpoints: serum viremia (DNA HBV) and serum viral particle load (HBsAg) for patients with HBV
- Virtual population characteristics  : The models use a body weight of 60 and 73 kg, total cardiac output of 5.9 and 6.5 L/min, tissue volume and blood flow parameters for adults females and males.
- Time scale  : Hour
- Biological scale  : Whole body

description: The PBPK model includes 16 tissue compartments (blood, lung, heart, kidney, muscle, skin, liver, brain, adipose, thymus, bone, small intestine, large intestine, spleen, pancreas and other) and a lymph node compartment connected to each other in an anatomical manner using blood and lymph flow. The ‘‘other’’ compartment represents carcass tissues e.g., thyroid, gonads etc.Each tissue compartment is further divided into vascular, endosomal, interstitial and cellular sub-compartments as shown in Figure 1

description:
Sodium thiopental is a rapid-onset short-acting barbiturate general anesthetic.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics. A PBPK model including 15 tissue compartments was implemented. The body for this model was composed of the following tissues: lung, adipose, bone, brain, heart, kidney, muscle, skin, liver, pancreas, spleen, and gut. The model also included compartments for venous blood and arterial blood, and a “rest-of-body” compartment that represented the remainder of the tissues not explicitly included in the model. The volume of the rest-of-body compartment was derived by subtracting the volumes of all other compartments from body weight, whereas the blood flow to this compartment was derived by subtracting the blood flows of all compartments entering into venous blood from the cardiac output.
contextOfUse:
- Treatment: Thiopental
- Species: Human
- Pathology: Anesthesia
- Biological context: The PBPK model was developed to investigate the impact of five commonly used tissue:plasma partition coefficient prediction methods (Poulin and Theil Method, Berezhkovskiy Method , Rodgers and Rowland Method, Schmitt Method, and PK-Sim Standard Method) on PBPK model predictions.
- Virtual population characteristics: The models use a body weight of 60 and 73 kg, total cardiac output of 5.9 and 6.5 L/min, tissue volume and blood flow parameters for females and males
- Time scale: Hour
- Biological scale: Whole body

description:
Cisplatin (orcis-diamminedichloroplatinum(II) [cis-PtCl2(NH3)2], CDDP)  is a chemotherapy drug that is used to treat a wide variety of cancers. In particular, it represents the standard-of-care in non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) therapy for advanced/metastatic stages in the form of platinum (Pt)-doublets (i.e. a Pt compound combined with a cytotoxic agent non Pt-based). It is generally accepted that nuclear DNA is the primary biological target of cisplatin: the formation of covalent cisplatin–DNA adducts can ultimately lead to cell cycle arrest and apoptosis.
Cisplatin pharmacokinetics is modeled here through a physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) approach, coupled to a low granularity pharmacodynamic (PD) model that accounts for citotoxic adducts formation and repair in the primary tumor (lung) and metastatic sites (liver, brain, bone and skin) as well. The resulting PBPK/PD model, shown in Fig. 1, integrates a mechanistic description of cisplatin administration, distribution and elimination with a model of its pharmacologic effect on the target sites, in order to provide a more comprehensive understanding of this drug behavior.
The applicability domain of the model is the simulation of the pharmacokinetic properties of cisplatin and its pharmacological effects in terms of DNA cross-links formation and repair (low granularity) in the primary tumor as well as in metastatic sites, following administration of cisplatin during treatment of advanced/metastatic LUAD subjects (intraperitoneal administration in xenografted mice and intravenous administration in humans).

description:
Two Anti NKG2A Monoclonal Antibodies are considered in this model.
Monalizumab is a potentially first-in-class immune checkpoint inhibitor targeting NKG2A receptors expressed on tumor infiltrating cytotoxic CD8+ T cells and NK cells.
KSQ mAb is a next generation of anti NKG2A monoclonal antibody.
Anti NKG2A Monoclonal Antibody are blocking antibody therapies that prevents the inhibition of CD8+ T cells and NK cell by tumor cells expressing HLA-E. By acting simultaneously on innate and adaptive immunity, these treatments may re-establish a broad anti-tumor response.
The developed model use ordinary differential equations.
The model consists of three compartments:
- (1) the central compartment, which comprises the    plasma and rapidly perfused tissues;
- (2) the peripheral compartment, which comprises all non disease, slowly perfused tissues;
- (3) the tumor compartment.
The drug is dosed intravenously to the central compartment and transports to the peripheral and tumor compartments.
First-order nonspecific clearance only occurs in the central compartment. In all compartments,bivalent drug binding to the membrane-bound NKG2A/CD94 heterodimer complex on NK and T cells was incorporated.
The steady-state target burden is modeled as a single pool of receptors in each compartment calculated from the number, fraction expressing, and expression level of NK and T cells in the compartment. The target synthesis rate is set by the steady-state concentration of the receptor complex in each compartment, and the receptor turnover rate, which is assumed to be the same in all compartments. Drug bound to the target is degraded intracellularly once internalized.

- Virtual population characteristics: None.

- Biological scale: Cell to molecular

description:
Brentuximab vedotin is an antibody-drug conjugate medication used to treat relapsed or refractory Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) and systemic anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALCL), a type of T cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma.Brentuximab-vedotin consists of an anti-CD30 antibody (cAC10) attached to potent tubulin polymerization inhibiting payload Monomethyl Auristatin E (MMAE), via a protease-sensitive dipeptide linker valine-citrulline (vc), using the conventional thiol based conjugation method. The linker is specifically designed to release MMAE by lysosomal cathepsin-B cleavage in target cancer cells, while maintaining stable linkage in the systemic circulation.
The model is a multiscale mechanism based PK/PD model, characterizing the biodisposition of ADC and payload at the cellular and physiological level, but also capable of providing translation of preclinical efficacy data to the clinic.

- Pathology:  HL and ALCL

- Virtual population characteristics: Model variability has been added to reflect observed variability in preclinical and clinical trial
- Time scale: day to months
- Biological scale: Cell

description:
Entecavir(ETV; formerly called BMS-200475, or SQ-34676)is an oral HBV nucleoside/tide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) approved for the treatment of chronic HBV infection. NRTIs inhibit the only known enzymatic target of HBV, the viral polymerase.
Based on ordinary differential equations, thisPBPKmodel focuses on the relationship between the drug's physicochemical properties and its distribution in the body (pharmacokinetics). It quantifies the drug concentration in the main tissues of the organism, including the liver, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, and lungs -considered as models-, based on administration regimen as well as virtual patient physiology. It does not explicitly consider treatment pharmacological effects (pharmacodynamics), but could be combined with such efficacy model. It considers factors such as drug-receptor interactions, drug metabolism, and clearance.The domain of application of this model is the description of the physiologically-based pharmacokinetic of Entecavir drug after oral administration inadults.
Related phenomena are: Perfusion-limited distribution throughout the body, oral administration and dissolution, drug transit in the gut, absorption in the small intestine, renal clearance and inhibition of viral polymerase in humans and in mice.

description:
Gefitinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) of first generation that targets the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR). The mutation of this receptor results in its constitutive activation and leads to uncontrolled cell proliferation which can subsequently be the cause of tumor development. In this regards, 16.4% of lung adenocarcinoma, a subtype of lung cancer, have been detected to harbor EGFR mutation. Gefitinib prevents the phosphorylation and activation of the EGFR receptor leading to reduced cell proliferation. Hence, gefitnib has been approved by the FDA in 2015 for the first-line treatment of patients with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) whose tumors have epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) exon 19 deletions or exon 21 (L858R) substitution mutations as detected by an FDA-approved test. Those two EGFR mutations are the most prevalent ones but other types of EGFR mutations exist. Rarer mutations as mutations in the exon 20 are insensitive to gefitnib.
Based on ordinary differential equations that takes into account physiological factors such as blood perfusion, tissue mass, chemical properties of the drug, the PBPK model is used to predict the distribution and elimination of a drug in the body. The PBPK model aims at predicting drug concentrations in specific organs such as the liver, kidneys, gastrointestinal tract, and lungs leading to the consideration of organ models. It also gives insights about drug drug interactions, and allows translation of results between population. It provides a more comprehensive understanding of how drugs are absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and excreted in different tissues.
This model accounts for the administration, distribution, metabolism and elimination of gefitinib both in humans and in mice as shown in Figure 1.

description:
Nevirapine is a medication used to treat and prevent HIV/AIDS.
The PBPK model used ordinary differential equations to describe well mixed tissues that were linked by the blood system. The model assumed perfusion rate–limited kinetics. A PBPK model including 15 tissue compartments was implemented. The body for this model was composed of the following tissues: lung, adipose, bone, brain, heart, kidney, muscle, skin, liver, pancreas, spleen, and gut. The model also included compartments for venous blood and arterial blood, and a “rest-of-body” compartment that represented the remainder of the tissues not explicitly included in the model. The volume of the rest-of-body compartment was derived by subtracting the volumes of all other compartments from body weight, whereas the blood flow to this compartment was derived by subtracting the blood flows of all compartments entering into venous blood from the cardiac output.

description: The model described here is mechanistic biomathematical model of molecular radiotherapy of thyroid disease. The general model consists of a set of differential equations describing the dynamics of different populations of thyroid cells with varying degrees of damage caused by radiotherapy (undamaged cells, sub-lethally damaged cells, doomed cells, and dead cells), as well as the dynamics of thyroglobulin and antithyroglobulin autoantibodies, which are important surrogates of treatment response.The model was used to fit the response dynamics (tumor/thyroid volumes, thyroglobulin and antithyroglobulin autoantibodies) observed in experimental studies of differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) and Graves’ disease treated with 131 I-radiotherapy.

description:
The present QSP model is an integrated cardiorenal model that builds on the Guyton and Bovendeerd models to link a detailed mechanistic representation of kidney function and volume homeostasis with the dynamics of cardiac function and remodeling. The model allows myocyte remodeling in response to changes in cardiac loading.
The primary functional modules and mechanisms of the mathematical model are schematically illustrated in Figure 1. This model is an extension of a previously published dynamic model of cardiac ventricular function, updated to account for myocyte adaptation and left ventricular remodeling in response to changes in mechanical loading. Integration with a pre-existing model of renal function and volume homeostasis allows for the simulation of interactions between cardiac and renal systems. The coupling between the cardiac and renal sections is mediated by blood volume, which is controlled by renal sodium and water excretion, and mean arterial pressure, which is calculated by the circulation submodel and influences renal perfusion and filtration.

description:
Maintaining mineral balance is a fundamental physiological process essential to human health. The QSP model is designed to represent the dynamic interactions within this system, offering valuable insights into pathological conditions and therapeutic interventions.
In health, blood calcium is kept remarkably stable through a delicate interplay between the parathyroid glands, bone, kidneys, and gut.
- Calcium Sensing:  Parathyroid glands detect low calcium and release Parathyroid Hormone (PTH).
- Systemic Response:  PTH, along with activated Vitamin D, orchestrates a multi-organ response to restore calcium levels, creating a precise negative feedback loop.
Several pathological conditions can impair this regulatory system, underscoring the need for predictive models to understand disease progression and inform treatment strategies.
- Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD):  Impaired kidney function reduces phosphate excretion and hinders Vitamin D activation, contributing to hypocalcemia. This stimulates excessive PTH production, leading to  CKD-Mineral and Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD)  .
- Parathyroid Disorders:
    - Primary Hyperparathyroidism:    Excessive PTH secretion from overactive parathyroid glands results in hypercalcemia.
  - Hypoparathyroidism:    Inadequate PTH production leads to hypocalcemia and elevated phosphate levels.

Associated changes in serum calcium and PTH were also considered of interest at the time and so justified the development of a 'systems' model that included bone remodeling and bone mineral (calcium and phosphate) homeostatic mechanisms. Other therapeutics (e.g., teriparatide) and disease states (kidney failure, parathyroid-related abnormalities) were also considered at the time to further inform the parameterization and estimation of the model

projectName: Triple Negative Breast Cancer - With Metastasis
description:
Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is an aggressive subtype of breast cancer characterized by the absence of estrogen receptors (ER), progesterone receptors (PR), and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2). This makes TNBC unresponsive to hormonal therapies or HER2-targeted treatments, leaving chemotherapy and immunotherapy as the primary treatment options. TNBC tends to have a higher risk of recurrence, rapid progression, and poorer prognosis compared to other breast cancer subtypes, though recent advances in immunotherapy and targeted treatments are improving outcomes.The QSP model were developed by drawing insights from previous studies on triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). Within this QSP model, the cancer module delineated the dynamics of cancer cell growth and death. The antigen module characterizes the release of both self and cancer-associated antigens from deceased cancer cells. Simultaneously, the antigen-presenting cells (APC) module outlined the process by which mature antigen-presenting cells (mAPC) captured antigens and migrated via lymphatic vessels to the tumor-draining lymph nodes (TdLNs). The module for effector T cells (Teff) and regulatory T cells (Treg) detailed the mAPC-mediated activation of naïve CD8+ and CD4+ T-cells, transforming them into Teffs and Tregs, respectively. Subsequent to activation, Teff and Treg cells exit the lymph nodes, circulate in the bloodstream, and, upon extravasation, localize to the tumor and other peripheral tissues. Within the tumor microenvironment, Teff cells engage in the direct elimination of cancer cells but may experience exhaustion due to suppressive actions by Tregs and the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction with tumor cells.
This QSP model uses components of a previously developed QSP model with single TNBC tumor and was extended in this study to incorporate multiple metastatic tumors. Because lung metastases are frequently observed in patients with TNBC and have a unique tumor microenvironment, the authors explicitly incorporated lung metastatic tumors in the QSP model by adding two lung tumor compartments that drain into a lung-draining lymph node compartment. In addition, they also incorporated an “other” tumor compartment representing any metastatic tumor other than the lung and an associated other tumor-draining lymph node compartment.

Within this QSP model, the cancer module delineated the dynamics of cancer cell growth and death. The antigen module characterizes the release of both self and cancer-associated antigens from deceased cancer cells. Simultaneously, the antigen-presenting cells (APC) module outlined the process by which mature antigen-presenting cells (mAPC) captured antigens and migrated via lymphatic vessels to the tumor-draining lymph nodes (TdLNs). The module for effector T cells (Teff) and regulatory T cells (Treg) detailed the mAPC-mediated activation of naïve CD8+ and CD4+ T-cells, transforming them into Teffs and Tregs, respectively. Subsequent to activation, Teff and Treg cells exit the lymph nodes, circulate in the bloodstream, and, upon extravasation, localize to the tumor and other peripheral tissues. Within the tumor microenvironment, Teff cells engage in the direct elimination of cancer cells but may experience exhaustion due to suppressive actions by Tregs and the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction with tumor cells.

description:
Colorectal cancer (CRC) is a type of cancer that starts in the colon or rectum, which are parts of the large intestine. It typically develops from precancerous polyps that grow on the inner lining of the colon or rectum. Over time, these polyps can become cancerous and invade surrounding tissues.
CRC is one of the most common cancers worldwide and is influenced by genetic, lifestyle, and environmental factors. Risk factors include age, diet, obesity, smoking, and a family history of the disease. Symptoms may include changes in bowel habits, blood in the stool, abdominal pain, and unexplained weight loss.
Early detection through screening, such as colonoscopy, significantly improves prognosis. Treatment options include surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and targeted therapies, depending on the stage and characteristics of the cancer.Cetuximab and panitumumab, both monoclonal antibodies that bind to EGFR and block EGF from binding. EGFRs are found in cell membranes in cells all over our body, and circulating epithelial growth factor (EGF) binds to this receptor and signals a cascade in the cell, resulting in cell proliferation. The increased growth rate in tumor cells is usually caused by multiple mutations, but a common mutation upregulates the number of EGFRs.

description:
The deterministic model in this study simulatesamyloid-beta (Aβ) and tau aggregationin Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and evaluatespassive and active immunizationagainst Aβ. It represents biochemical interactions usingordinary differential equations (ODEs)encoded inSBML. Key mechanisms includeAβ production and clearance,tau phosphorylation by GSK3β, andROS-driven feedback loops.
Key Findings from the Deterministic Model:
- Immunization  clears Aβ plaques  but has  minimal impact on soluble Aβ and tau tangles  .
- Early intervention  slows disease progression, but  delayed treatment  has limited benefits.
- GSK3β activity and ROS levels  influence both Aβ and tau aggregation.
- Repeated interventions prevent plaque buildup but may increase neuroinflammation.
The model helps identifykey parameters affecting disease progression, supporting the need formulti-target therapiesbeyond Aβ clearance.

- GSK3β and p53 interactions  : Activation of p53 leads to increased GSK3β activity, which enhances both  Aβ production and tau phosphorylation  .
- ROS production  : Aβ and tau aggregation contribute to increased oxidative stress, which further amplifies neurodegeneration.

- Repeated interventions prevent plaque accumulation  but may lead to excessive microglial activation, increasing  ROS production  , which could be neurotoxic.
- Aβ clearance alone may not be sufficient to halt disease progression  unless combined with therapies targeting tau pathology or oxidative stress.

projectName: Apoptosis in Diffuse Large B cell Lymphoma
description: Programmed cell death, known as apoptosis, plays a vital role in the development of multicellular organisms and the proper functioning of the immune system. The BCL2 family of proteins serves as the primary regulators of apoptosis with a membership exceeding twenty-five proteins. This family can be classified into three groups based on their protein structure and function: pro-apoptotic BCL2 proteins (such as BAX and BAK), anti-apoptotic BCL2 proteins (including BCL2, BCL-xL, MCL1, etc.), and BCL2 homology domain 3 (BH3)-only proteins (such as BID, BIM, PUMA, NOXA, etc.)1. In the initial stages of apoptosis, anti-apoptotic BCL2 proteins inhibit the oligomerization of BAX/BAK proteins at the mitochondrial outer membrane (MOM), preventing mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP). Activation of apoptosis involves the activation of BH3-only proteins, which can directly activate BAX/BAK through complex formation or indirectly activate BAX/BAK by sequestering anti-apoptotic BCL2 proteins. This sequestration leads to the release of BAX/BAK from complexes containing the anti-apoptotic BCL2 proteins. The activated BAX/BAK proteins subsequently induce MOMP, initiating apoptotic cell death.

projectName: Approach to an α-synuclein-based BST model of Parkinson's disease
description:
The paper describes a computational systems biology model for Parkinson's disease (PD) using a pragmatic application of Biochemical Systems Theory (BST). The model focuses on the aggregation of α-synuclein, a protein central to PD pathology, and its interaction with other cellular pathways such as:
The approach allows the integration of qualitative and semi-quantitative data to analyze system behavior and test hypotheses. Four disease perturbations are modeled, including elevated α-synuclein aggregation and overexpression, impaired dopamine packaging, and increased neurotoxin levels. Additionally, treatment strategies such as l-dopa therapy and MAO inhibition are evaluated.
Key insights include:
- Elevated α-synuclein and dopamine lead to increased ROS and proteasomal inhibition.
- Proteasomal and lysosomal activities are interlinked, compensating for dysfunctions in degradation pathways.
- Treatment effects show improvements in dopamine packaging but potential long-term cellular stress.
The model is validated against experimental literature and highlights emergent properties of PD, emphasizing its use in exploring therapeutic strategies and understanding disease mechanisms.

projectName: Metabolic Contribution to Photoreceptor Death in Retinitis
description:
Retinitis pigmentosa is a group of inherited degenerative eye diseases characterized by mutations in the genetic structure of the photoreceptors that leads to the premature death of both rod and cone photoreceptors.
In order to understand the process a simple ODEs model with 4 equations is implemented. This model accounts for healthy rod cells, sick rod cells, cones and "nutrients". The interaction between these four species allows to find several stable states that describes different biological conditions like first stage of RP, total blindness etc.

description:
Acute Myeloid Leukemia is a hematologic malignancy.It originates from a hematopoietic stem cell acquiring genetic mutations which increase the newly-appeared leukemic stem cell proliferation capacity and block the daughter cells's differentiation. The undifferentiated immature cells born from leukemic stem cells are designed as "leukemic blasts", which reside within the bone marrow and ultimately undergo extravasation into the bloodstream.The diagnosis of AML is posed when the patient shows more than 20% of leukemic blasts in the peripheral blood or bone marrow.
Nova's Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) model includes the leukemogenesis process from the leukemic stem cell to the final leukemic blast. The competition in terms of space in the bone marrow is described as an overcrowding phenomenon.
The AML model is composed of several modules in order to enable/disable several options, pathways, mechanisms, outcomes or treatments depending on the target context of use.

description: The model was constructed based on ordinary differential equations (ODEs).Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic autoimmune disease where the immune system mistakenly attacks healthy tissues in various parts of the body, leading to widespread inflammation and damage. SLE can affect multiple systems, including the skin, joints, kidneys, brain, and heart. Symptoms vary widely, often including fatigue, joint pain, skin rashes, and fever. The exact cause of SLE is not known, but it involves a combination of genetic, environmental, and hormonal factors. It is a physiological model (all the time variables are either numerical or normalized) that represents the five phases of SLE: the predisposition, the autoantibody manifestation, Clinical Podromes, the visible phase where the SLE is observable (and treated), and the flare and remission phase.

description: This paper presents a comprehensive model that simulates the progression and treatment of SARS-CoV-2 infection. By integrating viral load dynamics and immune responses in both plasma and lung tissues, the authors developed a quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) model to understand COVID-19 pathogenesis. They validated this model against clinical trial data involving SARS-CoV-2-targeting monoclonal antibodies and antivirals, accurately replicating both placebo and treatment responses. Additionally, the model was extended to predict hospitalization or mortality rates within a population. The study underscores the utility of QSP models in optimizing the timing of pharmacological interventions for COVID-19, potentially streamlining the development of effective treatments.
