description: The model was constructed based on ordinary differential equations (ODEs). Cellular biological behaviors of the ErbB system were described mainly by mass-action laws, from receptor-ligand binding to receptor dimerization, phosphorylation and degradation, then to activation of downstream signaling pathways, while signal transduction, feedback loop, and tumor growth were described by Hill functions.Five clinically approved therapeutics including T-DM1, T-DXd, lapatinib, pyrotinib and capecitabine were incorporated into the model and their PK profiles were characterized using standard ODE-based compartment models. To characterize the concentrations of drugs entering the tumor microenvironment, a simplifying assumption is used: drug exposures in the tumor are proportional to that in the blood, as described by tumor partition coefficients. For the two antibody drug conjugateds (ADCs), it was assumed that they could enter tumor microenvironment at a constant rate from central compartment.

description:
Apoptosis, also known as programmed cell death, is a crucial process in multicellular organisms. It plays a vital role in development, tissue homeostasis, and immune system function by eliminating unwanted or damaged cells. Apoptosis occurs through three main pathways: extrinsic, intrinsic, and perforin-dependent. Defective apoptosis can trigger the development and progression of cancer. Figure 1 give a schematic representation of the pathways and protein involved but to summarize:1. Extrinsic Pathway (Death Receptor Pathway):
- Trigger: Initiated by external signals from neighboring cells or the immune system.
- Key players: Death receptors (e.g., Fas, TNFR1) on the cell surface bind to specific ligands (e.g., Fas ligand, TNF-α).
- Signaling: Binding activates adaptor proteins, leading to the formation of a Death-Inducing Signaling Complex (DISC).
- Execution: DISC activates caspase-8, which directly cleaves and activates downstream caspases, ultimately leading to cell death.
2. Intrinsic Pathway (Mitochondrial Pathway):
- Trigger: Activated by internal stress signals like DNA damage, nutrient deprivation, or hypoxia.
- Key players: BCL-2 protein family members, including pro-apoptotic (e.g., Bax, Bak) and anti-apoptotic (e.g., Bcl-2, Bcl-xL) proteins.
- Signaling: Pro-apoptotic proteins induce mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization (MOMP).
- Execution: MOMP releases cytochrome c, which activates caspase-9 and downstream caspases, triggering cell death      3. Perforin-Dependent Pathway:
- Trigger: Mediated by cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) and natural killer (NK) cells of the immune system.
- Key players: Perforin, a protein secreted by CTLs/NKs, and granzymes, serine proteases released into the target cell.
- Action: Perforin creates pores in the target cell membrane, allowing granzymes to enter and activate caspases directly, leading to cell death.

description: COVID-19, which stands for Coronavirus Disease 2019, is a highly contagious respiratory illness caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus. The virus is spread through respiratory droplets produced when an infected person coughs or sneezes. These droplets can land in the mouths or noses of people who are nearby, or they can be inhaled into the lungs. Similar to other coronaviruses, SARS-CoV-2 initiates the infection process by binding to the host cells via its viral spike protein and then fusing with the cell membrane and releasing viral RNA into the cell. After genome replication and transcription, newly translated structural proteins and replicated RNA genomes are assembled in the endoplasmic reticulum- Golgi compartment, where new virus particles are formed and exit the host cell through exocytosis to complete the growth cycle.

description:
Psoriasis is a complex skin disorder driven by dysregulated immune responses. It is characterized by the excessive proliferation of keratinocytes, the predominant cell type in the epidermis. In psoriasis, an intricate interplay of genetic and environmental factors triggers an aberrant immune response. T cells, particularly Th1 and Th17 cells, play a pivotal role. These immune cells release proinflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-17 (IL-17), interleukin-23 (IL-23), and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α). These cytokines stimulate keratinocytes to multiply rapidly and produce an excessive amount of skin cells. In addition to this hyperproliferation, there's an increased vascularization of the skin, contributing to the redness and inflammation seen in psoriatic plaques. The persistent activation of the immune system and the imbalance of these cytokines are key drivers of the disease.
Psoriasis is a chronic persistent disease but may undergo periods of remission; currently though there is no cure. Narrow-band ultraviolet B (UVB) photo-therapy is one of just a few treatments that can clear psoriasis plaques over 8–12 weeks of therapy and induce a period of remission; the treatment is usually prescribed to patients with mild to moderate psoriasis.
The ODE-based model describes the interactions between immune stimuli, keratinocyte growth, differentiation and apoptosis in the onset of psoriasis lesions, regulation of psoriasis flares and plaque resolution during UVB phototherapy. It features two stable steady states (non-lesional and psoriatic skin); switching between them occurs through immune stimuli and UVB phototherapy.
contextOfUse:
- Treatment: UVB photo-therapy
- Specie: Human
- Pathology: Psoriasis
- Biological context: Proliferation and activation of stem cells and transit-amplifying cells is mediated by IL-22, IL-17, type I interferon and TNFα cytokines produced by the activated T cells and/or dendritic cells. In response to the increase in proliferation rate, keratinocytes increase their production of growth factors (GF) and chemokines that attract and activate dendritic cells producing more IL-23, which in turn activates more T cells, resulting in a positive feedback and self-sustaining loop. The model starts at the healthy state, until an immune stimulus is applied at  time_StartStim  . This stimulus consists in an increase of the dendritic cells activation rate by a constant amount  immuneStimAmount  during  immuneStimT  . Depending on the values of these parameters, the system will transition to psoriatic state. UVB photo-therapy starts at  startUVB  , with a gradually increasing dose of UVB 3 times a week for 8 weeks. Depending on the patient's sensitivity to UVB (parameter  uv_eff  ), the system will go back to healthy state after treatment.
- Virtual population characteristics: Distribution of sensitivity to UVB (parameter  uv_eff  ), magnitude and duration of immune stimulus (  immuneStimAmount  and  immuneStimT  ) extracted from the paper
- Time scale: day

description: Acute biological stress, such as severe infection or trauma, leads to the development of an acute inflammatory response. The goal of this response is to promote adaptation of the organism to stress, eliminate threats to survival such as pathogens, and promote tissue repair and healing. However, an excessive or inappropriate inflammatory response (systemic inflammatory response syndrome or SIRS) will lead to collateral tissue damage, organ dysfunction, a prolonged healing phase, or possibly death.Organisms have developed regulatory mechanisms to contain the molecular and cellular cascades initiated by excessive inflammation. In general, pro-inflammatory elements that are key to ridding organisms of large numbers of pathogens also mobilize a negative feedback, or anti-inflammatory response, which downregulatesthe initial inflammatory wave. Innate immune cells like monocytes/macrophages detect and respond to ‘‘danger signals’’ (e.g. pathogens, tissue damage) through pattern recognition receptors (PRR) expressed on their surface. Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) is the major PRR involved in the detection of Gram-negative bacteria and their associated endotoxins (e.g. Lipopolysaccharide, LPS; Lipid A).  Pathophysiological adaptations to regulate over-exuberant inflammation serve as an important mechanism for host protection against endotoxin shock. One of the classic examples of such a protective mechanism is endotoxin tolerance (ET), a phenomenon in which cells or organisms exposed to low concentrations of endotoxin (e.g. LPS) enter into a transient unresponsive state and are unable to respond to further challenges with endotoxin.Roy et al. (2007) on the paper A mathematical model of acute inflammatory response to endotoxin challenge  describes an 8-state differential equation model of the acute inflammatory response system to endotoxin challenge at 3, 6 and 12 mg/kg in rats.
contextOfUse:

- Pathology: Endotoxin challenges at 3, 6 and 12 mg/kg to evaluate systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS)
- Biological context:
    - The primary objective of the article work was to ground the intermediate inflammatory response model in experimental data, thereby providing a model that captures the dynamic blood concentrations of the major pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines resulting from introduction of an endotoxin challenge (P(t)) in rats.
  - Main hypotheses: At basal condition, the system is considered anti-inflammatory. Il6 is predominantly considered a pro-inflammatory mediator. The second surge in IL10 production is attributed to tissue damage D. The dynamics of this D-induced effect are captured by the variable YIL10 (data-motivated).

description:
EGFR belongs to the HER/ErbB family of ligand-activated receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs). EGFR can be activated by binding to different ligands including epidermal growth factor (EGF), transforming growth factor (TGF-α), betacellulin (BTC), epiregulin (EPR) and amphiregulin (AR). Ligand binding to the extracellular domain leads to the receptor dimerization (formation of homodimers or heterodimers) and subsequent autophosphorylation of the receptor on multiple tyrosine residues in the intracellular domain of each monomer. These phosphorylated tyrosines serve as speciﬁc binding sites for adapter proteins that lead to the activation of multiple signaling pathways including mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) and phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase (PIK3).
The binding of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) and IGF-2 to the extracellular domain of IGF1R induces the receptor tyrosine kinase activity with resultant autophosphorylation at speciﬁc tyrosine residues. The phosphorylated receptor triggers the association of the Insulin Receptor Substrates (IRSs) and other adaptor proteins with the subsequent activation of the MAPK and PIK3 cascades.
The biological relationship between the proteins involved in EGFR and IGF1R pathways and the downstream MAPK and PIK3 networks has been modeled by means of a set of ordinary differential equations (ODEs) to study the time behavior of the overall system, and the functional interdependences among the receptors, the proteins and kinases involved.

description:
Neutropenia refers to low levels of neutrophils in the blood. Neutrophils are a sub-type of  granulocytes, which are immune cells produced in the bone marrow. Neutrophils mainly intervenes to neutralize infections by virus or bacteria. In case of neutropenia, the patient is more likely to be infected and not to be able to be cured by himself. In some case of severe neutropenia, even bacteria normally tolerated by the body con lead to an infection.
Neutropenia can typically occur for patients undergoing chemotherapy, since this therapy may destroy both cancer cells and neutrophils. Several treatments can be used to treat neutropenia in the case of chemotherapy. Here, we focus on two categories:
- Corticosteroids:  These drugs can suppress (lessen) your body’s immune response that’s causing the destruction of your neutrophils.
- Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF):  These drugs promotes the production of immune cells and especially neutrophils.  G-CSF is often administrated while giving chemotherapy.
The number of cells (and in particular, neutrophils) decreases after a chemotherapy cycle and then slowly increase to its basal level. The point where the amount of cells is the lowest is called nadir. While the term nadir is used in reference to all blood cell counts, the concern during nadir is the drop in immune cells. After the nadir, the blood cell count will start to rise, decreasing the risk of danger and recovering to a safe level before the next treatment.

description:
Liver ischemia reperfusion injury (IRI) is a type of tissue damage that occurs when the blood supply to the liver is interrupted and then restored. This can happen during liver surgery, transplantation, or shock.
During ischemia, the lack of blood flow deprives the liver of oxygen and nutrients. When blood flow is restored, oxygen and nutrients rush back into the liver, but this can also lead to damage. This is because the reoxygenated blood contains harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can damage cells.
The model is a phenomenological model for perfusion with hepatic metabolism of relevant for IRI.

description:
The mathematical model incorporates pancreatic cancer cells, pancreatic stellate cells, three major classes of immune effector cells CD8+ T cells, natural killer cells, helper T cells. IL-2, IFN-γ and TGF-β concentration are also modeled but are defined using quasi-steady-state approximations.
Speciﬁc biological assumptions are ﬁrst presented as follows:
(1) The cancer cells grow logistically in the absence of an immune response.
(2) Both NK-cells and CD8+ T cells are capable of killing cancer cells.
(3) Both NK-cells and CD8+ T cells are activated by cancer cells.
(4) Both NK-cells and CD8+ T cells eventually become inactivated after some number of interactions with tumor cells.

description:
The complement system is a group of proteins that work together to protect the body from infection and inflammation. It is part of the innate immune system, which is the body's first line of defense against infection.
The model consists of a system of eighteen nonlinear ordinary differential equations and consider rates of complement activation describing  the lectin and alternative pathways.

- Treatment: None
- Specie: Human
- Pathology: Complement system
- Biological context: The model is a reduced order complement network that described the initiation of the alternative and lectin pathways, and the downstream integration of the lectin and classical pathways (Fig 1). A trigger event initiated the lectin pathway (encoded as a logical rule), which activated the cleavage of C2 and C4 into C2a, C2b, C4a and C4b, respectively. The model did not explicitly model classical pathway activation mediated by the C1 protein. Instead it described the integration of the lectin and classical pathways at the level of C2 and C4 activation and the formation of the Classical Pathway (CP) C3 convertase (C4aC2b).

description: Glucose is an indispensable fuel for all cells and organs, but at the same time leads to problems at high concentrations. As a consequence, blood glucose is controlled in a narrow range to guarantee constant supply and on the other hand avoid damages associated with elevated glucose levels. The liver is the main organ controlling blood glucose by (i) releasing newly synthesized or stored glucose in the blood stream when blood glucose is low (ii) using and storing glucose when blood glucose is elevated. These processes are regulated by hormones, in particular insulin, glucagon and epinephrine.The developed model present a detailed kinetic model of glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogen metabolism in human hepatocytes integrated with the hormonal control of these pathways by insulin, glucagon and epinephrine

description: Atopic dermatitis (AD) is a chronic inflammatory condition of the skin characterized by recurrent eczematous lesions and intense itch which can profoundly impair quality of life. The pathophysiology of AD is complex and involves local and systemic immune dysregulation, genetic susceptibility, environmental factors and microbiome effects. AD is not a homogeneous disease and encompasses a variety of endotypes and phenotypes in the different age, ethnic, etc. groups.Nova’s atopic dermatitis (AD) model accounts for the interplay between the skin barrier and the immune system. It describes the key pathways and biomarkers influencing disease course, including the mechanisms driving the immune response dysregulation, the loss of skin barrier integrity and the perturbation of the skin microbiome.
contextOfUse:
- Treatments : Topical and systemic immunosuppressors (corticosteroids, NSAID), Other topical treatments (e.g. calcineurin inhibitors), Biologics (monoclonal antibody) and more generally, topical or systemic investigational treatment
- Species: Human
- Pathology: Atopic Dermatitis
- Biological context: The model is formulated as a set of biological reactions and ordinary differential equations that were developped to quantify the relationship between trial design options and the expected efficacy profiles while taking into account the patients’ immune profiles and thereby addressing biomarker related trial design choices. The model was calibrated quantitative relationships in skin biomarkers, clinical data for standard of care (SoC) and OM-85 treatment efficacy.
- Virtual population characteristics: Pediatric population, Mild, moderate or severe AD patients, Specific AD endotypes (e.g. IL -17/11-22 high)
- Time scale: weeks to years
metrics:
- Variables (21): major biomarkers of AD lesional skin (filaggrin, S. aureus in the skin microbiome, skin barrier integrity), major immune biomarkers (cytokines and chemokines characterizing the type-1, 2, 17, 22, and regulatory responses), immune cells (Langerhans cells, Inflammatory Dendritic Epidermal Cells, Treg, type-2 innate lymphocyte cells), antibodies (polyclonal IgE)
- Reactions (36): release, activation, production, degradation, and death of the different variables.
- Ordinary Differential Equations (2): Regulation of skin barrier integrity, regulation of S. aureus proportion among pathogens.
- Parameters (336): release, activation, production, degradation, and death rates, scaling factors, parameters related to Hill functions
- Compartments (2): Lesional Skin, Venous
- Events (7): Start and Stop treatments administration
standOutFeatures:
- Possibility to study different treatment regimen and bitherapies
- Outputs of interest:
SCORAD: Converted from EASI
EASI: Patient EASI (Eczema Area and Severity Index).
Skin barrier integrity (SkinBI): Dimensionless quantity between 0 and 1 representing the skin barrier integrity.
Inflammatory biomarkers
